body.label: Definition 1
body.definition: Let G be a group with identity element e. A function l : G -> R is a pseudo-length function on G if the following four conditions hold: nonnegativity, identity, symmetry, and triangle inequality.
body.name: PseudoLength

source.text: Let G be a group with identity element e. A function l : G -> R is a pseudo-length function on G if the following four conditions hold: nonnegativity, identity, symmetry, and triangle inequality.
source.label: Definition 1

body.text: The nonnegativity condition is included explicitly so that the codomain can be the usual real numbers in a formalization. Equivalently, one may take the codomain to be the set of nonnegative real numbers and omit the separate nonnegativity axiom.

body.label: Definition 2
body.definition: Let G be a group with identity element e. A pseudo-length function l : G -> R is a length function if, for every g in G, g != e implies 0 < l(g).
body.name: IsLength

source.text: Let G be a group with identity element e. A pseudo-length function l : G -> R is a length function if, for every g in G, g != e implies 0 < l(g).
source.label: Definition 2

body.label: Definition 3
body.definition: Let G be a group. A pseudo-length function l : G -> R is homogeneous if, for every g in G and every integer n in Z, l(g^n) = |n| l(g). In particular, for every g in G, homogeneity gives l(g^2)=2l(g).
body.name: IsHomogeneousPseudoLength

source.text: Let G be a group. A pseudo-length function l : G -> R is homogeneous if, for every g in G and every integer n in Z, l(g^n) = |n| l(g). In particular, for every g in G, homogeneity gives l(g^2)=2l(g).
source.label: Definition 3

proof.text: Fix x,y in G. We first prove l(yxy^{-1}) <= l(x). Let n be a positive integer. By homogeneity, n l(yxy^{-1}) = l((yxy^{-1})^n). The group identity (yxy^{-1})^n = yx^n y^{-1} gives n l(yxy^{-1}) = l(yx^n y^{-1}). By the triangle inequality, l(yx^n y^{-1}) <= l(y) + l(x^n) + l(y^{-1}). By symmetry, l(y^{-1})=l(y). By homogeneity, l(x^n)=n l(x). Hence n l(yxy^{-1}) <= 2l(y)+n l(x). Dividing by n gives l(yxy^{-1}) <= l(x)+2l(y)/n. Since this holds for every positive integer n and 2l(y)/n tends to 0, l(yxy^{-1}) <= l(x). For the reverse inequality, define z := yxy^{-1}. Then x=y^{-1}zy. Applying the inequality already proved with z in place of x and y^{-1} in place of y gives l(x)=l(y^{-1}zy) <= l(z)=l(yxy^{-1}). The two inequalities imply equality.

Define KtwoN to be n minus the sum over i from 1 to 2 * n of (1 + eps i) / 2.

proof_steps.proof_method: By the definition of S_{2n} as the sum of the first 2n Rademacher variables.

S2n = eps1 + ... + eps2n
the sum over integers i from 1 to 2n of (1 + eps applied to i) / 2 = n + S2n / 2.
proof_steps.proof_method: Split the finite sum into the sum of the constant terms and the sum of the eps_i terms.

the sum over integers i from 1 to 2n of (1 + eps applied to i) / 2 = n + S2n / 2
KtwoN = -S2n / 2.
proof_steps.proof_method: Substitute the evaluated sum into the definition of KtwoN and simplify.

proof_steps.claim: f applied to 0 and n is less than or equal to the expectation of f applied to MtwoN and KtwoN
f applied to 0 and n is less than or equal to the expectation of f applied to S2n and -S2n / 2.
proof_steps.proof_method: Substitute MtwoN = S_{2n} and KtwoN = -S_{2n}/2 into the iterated inequality.

proof.text: Lemma 4 gives f(m,k) <= 1/2 f(m-1,k)+1/2 f(m+1,k-1), equivalently f(m,k) <= E[f(m+epsilon,k-(1+epsilon)/2)] for a Rademacher variable epsilon. Starting at (0,n) and applying this successively with epsilon_1,...,epsilon_{2n} gives f(0,n) <= E[f(M_{2n},K_{2n})], where M_{2n}=epsilon_1+...+epsilon_{2n} and K_{2n}=n-sum_{i=1}^{2n}(1+epsilon_i)/2. Then M_{2n}=S_{2n}, and the sum equals n+S_{2n}/2, so K_{2n}=-S_{2n}/2. Substitution proves the claim.

source.text: Let G be a group. Let l : G -> R be a homogeneous pseudo-length function. Fix x,y in G and set c:=[x,y]. Define f(m,k):=l(x^m c^k). Let n be a natural number. Let epsilon_1,...,epsilon_{2n} be independent random variables with P(epsilon_i=1)=P(epsilon_i=-1)=1/2. Define S_{2n}:=epsilon_1+...+epsilon_{2n}. Since 2n is even, S_{2n} is an even integer, and -S_{2n}/2 is integer-valued.
source.label: Lemma 5

proof.text: Fix x,y in G and define c:=[x,y]. Let n be a positive integer. By homogeneity, n l(c)=l(c^n)=f(0,n), where f(m,k):=l(x^m c^k). By Lemma 5, f(0,n) <= E[f(S_{2n},-S_{2n}/2)]. For every even integer r, f(r,-r/2)=l(x^r c^{-r/2}) <= l(x^r)+l(c^{-r/2}) by the triangle inequality. By homogeneity, l(x^r)=|r|l(x) and l(c^{-r/2})=|r|l(c)/2. Therefore E[f(S_{2n},-S_{2n}/2)] <= E(|S_{2n}|)(l(x)+l(c)/2). By Lemma 6, E(|S_{2n}|) <= sqrt(2n). Thus n l(c) <= sqrt(2n)(l(x)+l(c)/2). Dividing by n and letting n tend to infinity gives l(c) <= 0. By nonnegativity, l(c)=0. Since x and y were arbitrary, the result follows.

proof_steps.source_claim: There exists a unique function barL : G_ab -> R such that l = barL composed with pi_ab. Moreover, barL is a homogeneous pseudo-length function on the abelian group G_ab.

proof.text: Define barL(pi_ab(g)):=l(g). If pi_ab(g)=pi_ab(h), then h^{-1}g in [G,G]. By Lemma 8, l(h^{-1}g)=0. Since g=h(h^{-1}g), the triangle inequality gives l(g) <= l(h). Similarly, g^{-1}h in [G,G] gives l(h) <= l(g). Hence l(g)=l(h), so barL is well-defined. The identity l=barL o pi_ab holds by definition, and surjectivity of pi_ab proves uniqueness. The pseudo-length axioms for barL follow from those for l and from pi_ab being a homomorphism; for instance, barL(ab)=barL(pi_ab(gh))=l(gh)<=l(g)+l(h)=barL(a)+barL(b). Nonnegativity, identity, symmetry, and homogeneity are analogous.

proof.text: By Lemma 9, l factors through a homogeneous pseudo-length function barL:G_ab -> R. By Lemma 11, barL factors through the torsion-free quotient A:=G_ab/T(G_ab). Let tildeL:A -> R be the induced homogeneous pseudo-length function. By Lemma 12, tildeL extends to a seminorm on VQ:=A tensor_Z Q. By Lemma 13, there exist a real Banach space B and a canonical map VQ -> B preserving the seminorm. Define phi:G -> B to be the composite G -> G_ab -> A -> VQ -> B. Each arrow is a group homomorphism when B is regarded additively, so phi is a group homomorphism. By construction, l(g)=||phi(g)||_B. If l is a length function and phi(g)=0, then l(g)=0, hence g=e. Thus the kernel of phi is trivial, so phi is injective.

proof_steps.label: homogeneous.root.corollary-15.proof.root.reverse.verify_length_axioms
proof_steps.name: homogeneous_root_corollary_15_proof_root_reverse_verify_length_axioms
proof_steps.claim: lengthFromNorm is a homogeneous length function on G.
lengthFromNorm(g) is defined as the norm of the image of g in V
The natural map G -> V is injective
The chosen norm on V is definite
For every nonzero g in G, lengthFromNorm(g) > 0.
proof_steps.proof_method: If g is nonzero, injectivity of the natural map G -> V implies that the image of g is nonzero in V; definiteness of the norm then gives that its norm, hence lengthFromNorm(g), is positive.

proof_steps.claim: lengthFromNorm(g) is defined as the norm of the image of g in V
The chosen norm on V satisfies norm(0)=0
lengthFromNorm(0) = 0.
proof_steps.proof_method: Evaluate the defining norm expression at 0 and use the norm axiom that the norm of 0 is 0.

proof_steps.claim: lengthFromNorm(g) is defined as the norm of the image of g in V
The chosen norm on V is symmetric
For every g in G, lengthFromNorm(-g) = lengthFromNorm(g).
proof_steps.proof_method: The natural map sends -g to the negative of the image of g; apply symmetry of the norm.

proof_steps.claim: lengthFromNorm(g) is defined as the norm of the image of g in V
The chosen norm on V is subadditive
For every g h in G, lengthFromNorm(g + h) <= lengthFromNorm(g) + lengthFromNorm(h).
proof_steps.proof_method: The natural map sends g + h to the sum of the images of g and h; apply subadditivity of the norm.

proof_steps.claim: lengthFromNorm(g) is defined as the norm of the image of g in V
The chosen norm on V is homogeneous
For every integer n and every g in G, lengthFromNorm(n • g) = |n| * lengthFromNorm(g).
proof_steps.proof_method: The natural map sends n • g to n times the image of g; apply homogeneity of the norm.

proof_steps.claim: For every nonzero g in G, lengthFromNorm(g) > 0
lengthFromNorm(0) = 0
For every g in G, lengthFromNorm(-g) = lengthFromNorm(g)
For every g h in G, lengthFromNorm(g + h) <= lengthFromNorm(g) + lengthFromNorm(h)
For every integer n and every g in G, lengthFromNorm(n • g) = |n| * lengthFromNorm(g)
lengthFromNorm is a homogeneous length function on G.
proof_steps.proof_method: These are exactly the positivity, zero, symmetry, triangle inequality, and homogeneity axioms for a homogeneous length function on G.

proof_steps.text: This is positive on nonzero g by injectivity and definiteness of the norm, and the norm gives l(0)=0, l(-g)=l(g), l(g+h)<=l(g)+l(h), and l(ng)=|n|l(g).
